title: Claude Cookbook | Hacker News
excerpt: Gotta be honest, almost every "how to use AI" resource seems pointless to me. I'm either going to ask the AI how to do it, or if it's about using the AI then we can just bake it into the harness or wait for Anthropic/OpenAI to do it for me because they're always trivial.
content_html:
Gotta be honest, almost every "how to use AI" resource seems pointless to me. I'm either going to ask the AI how to do it, or if it's about using the AI then we can just bake it into the harness or wait for Anthropic/OpenAI to do it for me because they're always trivial.

All of these resources on agentic workflows, managing agent memory, harness engineering, etc. appear to just be theatre to me.


Remember in 2023 when people thought "prompt engineering" would be the new software engineering and invested tons of time into learning CoT, ReAct, thread-of-thoughts, etc?

Those were mostly obviated by reasoning models and harness updates by 2024.

It seems pointless to invest energy into the latest/greatest AI technique or framework when they're going to either be absorbed or replaced on a 3 month cycle.


Isn't it clear that some people are better at working with/prompting LLMs than other people? Or is the idea that what you write to them and how you use them doesn't matter, it's all up to the model/harness? To me this seems clear, so then clearly this is a skill, which typically is called "prompt engineering". Specifically CoT or the other things you mention wasn't referred to as "prompt engineering" as far as I know, that skill is more about how you communicate with the LLMs and how you use them, rather than what specific processes/workflows/technologies you use.


I actually think that good prompting MOSTLY comes from good writing skills in general. Being able to more clearly state things to an agent, knowing what pieces of context are entirely unnecessary and which are important, having a larger vocabulary helps too.

Of course, there are other areas that can improve model output (Direction rather than open-ended assistance requests, using keywords + plugins that help, the "your output should include: " style prompting).

A few of us run almost the same exact setup at my shop (Base Claude Code w/ SuperPowers + a context repository) and the models are somewhat unhelpful to some, and give meaningful output to others. The only correlation I notice is that their prompts are no-good. Not from a meta "prompt" engineering standpoint, but from a general English 101 standpoint.

"dudde no i wanted the function to return 3 things. not like that. do it again"

VS something like

"Modify the "renderThreeVars()" function signature to accept another variable called "z" and add it to the return statement at line 64."

Obvious exaggeration, but you get the point.


Why not open-ended assistance requests?

I ask it all the time about whether X is feasible, how we can get started on Y, and to investigate issue Z.

It is working great for me in a >100k LOC project.

Perhaps this works less well with weaker models. I suspect the people who say Qwen 3.6 27B is working well, are using prompts like "modify the renderThreeVars() function in rendering.py".


As part of a previous job I needed to audit internal AI usage from a largely non-technical employee population.

The prompts were, predictably, really bad. Broken English, sentence fragments, vague requests, lack of context. Yet somehow, the users always got the answer they were looking for. It might have taken a few extra turns with questions from the model, but the end result was the same.

It's humbling, but a flowery, carefully crafted prompt is at best slightly more efficient than a "CAN A DOG BE EATIN SUN FLOWER SEED?" peasant prompt.


You call that a peasant prompt, but it's actually almost perfect. Couple notes, but it's 95% of the way there. "Can a dog eat sunflower seed" is probably the perfect version, just 1 extraneous word in this version.

Unless the user wanted to know if a cat could eat sunflower seed or something.


To be pedantic, these types of prompts work best when in first-person/roleplaying. So the "perfect" prompt here would be something like, "I'm a dog and I just ate 20 grams of salted sunflower seeds with the shell on. Because I'm a dog I sometimes eat things without thinking about it. I'm worried about the short and long-term physiological consequences of what I've just done..."


I copied your prompt verbatim to ChatGPT and to Google.

ChatGPT kept the charade for all of one sentence. Then it dropped to talking about "your dog" the rest of the way. It even starts the final paragraph with "if, instead, you mean you (a human) ate them...", and finishes with the question "is this about an actual dog or yourself?"

Google did consistently refer to me as a dog, but its entire focus was on the steps "your human" should take, no advice for the dog itself.

In both cases, it looks like the first person roleplay was entirely inconsequential for the usefulness of the output. I think the current gen AIs have outgrown this trick and you can safely forget about it.


I think some people who are better at "prompting" even without domain knowledge could be better at getting LLM agents to produce good results than people with good domain knowledge but without the skills to prompt well. Just a hypothesis though, would be fun to try it out for real sometime :)


I feel the same way about "prompt engineering" as I feel regarding the term "parkour" - you know, running and jumping on stuff.

Are people really putting on their resumes that they are capable of reading and writing and appropriately defining and limiting context? That's all prompt engineering is - it's being able to communicate effectively and elucidate your objectives.

Congratulations to all you English majors out there, you're about to make $350K/year.


What's clear is that there is a lot of hype around LLM and people who were previously valued for their IC are now in the business of shilling.


RL has basically killed prompt engineering. You still need to provide the right context and process, but how you communicate with them beyond that is no longer so important.


> Or is the idea that what you write to them and how you use them doesn't matter, it's all up to the model/harness?

Yes, it is. Source: had models inplement complex things from scratch and bullshit regardless of whether it was a one-line prompt or a detailed "SOTA witchcraft magic spells that are guaranteed to work"


It's not really a skill. The models are at this point smarter than you are, so the idea that you can prompt them "better" is laughable really when discussing frontier models.

It's like imagining you could "prompt" Richard Feynman to be smarter at Physics.

That is, for 99.9% of engineers, if you want the model to do a code review of your project, the best solution is to just ask Fable, "Hey Fable, do a code review of this project." Throwing in extra text like "think like a senior engineer", "ensure you focus on DRY principles, KISS, self documenting code, etc", doesn't make a difference.

These sorts of tricks used to work with dumber models, but now, like I said before, it's like thinking you can prompt Linus Torvalds into writing better C than he already can do.


"DRY" and "KISS" are, possibly, the least interesting principles to which a software designer might adhere.


FWiW

> it's like thinking you can prompt Linus Torvalds into writing better C++ than he already can do.

  Linus Torvalds, the inventor (and beloved dictator) of Linux, has always been quite harsh about C++ and why he rejects it for Linux kernel development. He’s not just been very vocal about it, but also brought up some arguments against the use of C++ that are worth reviewing in detail.

~ https://medium.com/@jankammerath/linus-torvalds-critique-of-...


I mean, the point stands.

The models are beyond expert level in many areas at this point.

Do you really believe that adding extra junk to your prompt is going to make the model write code better than it does already?

Again, imagine going to Terrence Tao and "prompting" him to get better at Maths, do you think you can do it? What prompt would you give to him to make him produce better maths. Unless you're already a world-leading Mathematician I think you would find it hard.


> I mean, the point stands. The models are beyond expert level in many areas at this point.

It's not that the models aren't smart or whatever; we know they're extremely capable.

There's always going to be value in being able to clearly communicate what you want the model to do, especially when the context is lacking.


I find this very much untrue in my practice and my testing, where fable prompted to do a review found surface level issues, while prompting along the lines of "assume it's wrong, prove it's correct" found much more in depth and real issues.


I still believe in writing good prompts or good instructions. Bad prompts can sometimes blow up the bill. A poorly written spec can waste a lot of tokens


Definitely. But the special knowledge how to talk to a certain model is usually not worth it.

Being able to write clear, always is.


There was an article by Vercel on how ineffective tool calling is compared to just a single md file with clearly defined instructions. They showed a clever way on how to use compressed indexes. My experience with tool calling was similar to what Vercel described, and I spent hours trying to perfect it. Since reading Vercel’s findings, Claude.md and Agents.md, maybe a Project.md it’s all I use.


Right now I feel like I'm getting so much value out of working daily with Fable, but I'd be embarrassed to share my sessions because they're pretty much how I would talk to a colleague. Spelling mistakes and half-baked thoughts included. Gone are the formal sounding prompts and specs I was proudly writing 6 months ago. But it's getting the results.


Not only that, but all of this tooling around models has such a short shelf life as the models themselves grow in capabilities, they absorb the tooling. We've already seen it over and over again.


Amen on both your and OP's comments.

These tools work pretty well out-of-the-box. I'm sure I could squeeze out better token usage or streamline some tool calls, but it's not something I really want to focus on. Just like I don't want to endlessly configure my IDE, I don't really have patience with spending time on anything besides actually building something.


>I'm either going to ask the AI how to do it

LLMs seem terrible at using LLMs in harnesses. Have you seen how they rot their context with the stuff they put in .md files if you let them?

You'd have to have the LLMs search, and thus these resources could be for them more than you


Yes, but I have had moderate luck with creating an "agent-instructor" skill that has strict instructions around keeping language strong, unambiguous, concise, and always presenting me with exact diffs to review before writing anything.

Another thing in it is a strict line count. Any increase in line count requires my approval. That last one is important because it plays well with two biases: models don't tend to create long lines so they won't try to cheat that way, and they're strongly inclined to keep churning out lines so I take that away from them.


Did you tell Claude "make an agent instructor skill, whatever you think is good, go for it," or did you use the knowledge you had gained about how AI works and how to write good instructions for it?


I suspect certain workflows like langchain and others like it will retain usefulness into the future. Having deterministic steps before and after the llm is the way to go for anything that might be potentially harmful. Which I guess is what the harness does, but why be limited to a generic harness when we can use it to make specific ones for our needs.


Thanks for saying the quiet part out loud. Everytime I do a demo it seems like a waste of time compared to just actually building.

Unless I'm doing something super complicated even taking time to set things up like subagents, etc. seems like a waste compared to just building.

The only things that really seem beneficial (for Claude Code) seems to be learning to set up loops, memory and finding relevant MCP servers.


Exactly. I think we all have to come to this conclusion ourselves, because the message we’ve been getting from the LLM companies is “sweet user, you DO add value to the LLM, you gave it that custom subagent, remember?”, and then they go and roll that idea into the next version. Once they do that a few times you go “eh why would I bother, this will be a default soon. I’ll just prompt it like normal”.

Just use the vanilla settings.


Yea i do the same. I can't be bothered to watch videos and courses on how to use claude better when I can simply ask it myself.


> I'm either going to ask the AI how to do it

First you have to know that "it" exists and is possible. A cookbook like this introduces readers to concepts and features they didn't know were there in the first place.


It was more important with earlier models, as they were less RL'd to golden paths, so the context could steer them more.

Now process engineering is more important than prompt engineering.


This is how I've gone about it with my Shopify site.

I appreciate there may be more efficient ways to do things if you're an actual developer or engineer but I'm not and the tools I've been able to build so far have been both fun to make and add value to our workflow as a small 2 person ecommerce business.

I do at least have a background in web as a designer for many years and having worked with developers I can at least spec and understand how a product might work which is one thing Claude/AI isn't hugely helpful with and often it's the testing and QA phase as always where the problems and shortcomings expose themselves.


Ok, thanks, because I looked at one example and was like, “I am supposed to take someone’s OpenAPI doc and translate it back to English for the model?” And if they’re implying I should have an AI do that, why don’t they just build that step into the models rather than having someone prompt an AI to write these half-ass docs?


if you know how to ask Ai about how to use Ai, you already know how to use Ai

the point of guides is to provide assurance for people unfamiliar to the process in the first place


If the bar is knowing how to type a question into a box, I'm confident almost everyone is better off starting with that then reading a "cookbook" that starts with installing python packages.


I've see people write "broooo pleaseeee!" into the box. Something about the universe providing better fools.


I think the main thing of interest in the linked site is the dates. You can quickly get a view of what was possible and when.


You don't learn about progress if you don't take part in it.

Before /goal was ralph-wiggum it was def an interesting learning experience, it was interesting to see how Claude became a lot better doing this itself but it still took 6 Month and more.

You can wait and sit it out and suddenly you get fired if you miss the point when to start spending more time and energy on topics like this.


I've been using Matt Pocock's skills at home, and they seem pretty great. He makes a great case for them vs other skills in his videos. I don't remember the exact arguments, but basically they're designed to be called by the user instead of called automatically, and that makes them take less context merely because they exist.

I've also found that they tend to coerce the programmer into thinking about the end result, rather than try to push them out of that role. His grill skills are about making sure the actual requirements are known, and his prototype skill can help explore things that need to be experienced to make a hard decision on.


Fascinating! Makes me wonder if they were chosen to maximize the contrast between the before and after shots, just to "prove that it "works"".

Personally I prefer the before shots all the way down.


I was hoping too, because I use a Claude project for recipes and it’s great for suggestions and adapting recipes for what’s in the pantry.


Does anybody here have a frontend development workflow that works well with coding agents? In my experience coding agents ship buggy, broken, incomplete, or awkward frontend features at a way higher frequency than they do for backend features. The reason seems obviously to be the difference in verifiability between the two. Basic test suites dont seem to help much. I assume something like Garry Tan’s gstack is the right direction but I dont know if that particular tool is mature enough to adopt yet. I was surprised too see here on HN recently that gemini 3.5 flash may outperform Opus/gpt-5.5 on frontend tasks (maybe because of Gemini’s supposed edge w.r.t. multimodality? or maybe it understands Chrome more deeply?), can anyone here second that take?


There may be good options coupled with agents like Claudes Front End Design skill, I haven't used it.

I have been using Magic Patterns which is very good at generating initial prototypes, especially when driven by an agent familiar with it's facilities and constraints.

I assume the service is just a wrapper plus scaffolding but I like it. You have to push it though to get anything particularly creative but it's good at standard front end designs.

https://www.magicpatterns.com/

That's not necessarily what you're after because I only use it for piloting stuff, not for adhoc feature addition and fixes.


Agents aren't great at complex async state updates, mostly because it's hard to represent what's happening in their context. They're fine at creating complex components in isolation and implementing decoupled stuff like animations.


After all this effort of not opening the models, then trying to forbid people to use open models and all the rubbish their CEO keeps talking about common people I am done with Anthropic.

I can't use it on good faith Same goes for grok, chat gpt and others all involved in killing people and monopolising the market.


That sounds like a great idea. Let's stop killing people altogether. It's almost entirely unnecessary.


I thought it would be a litteral cookbook on how to make plausible/feasible recipes with LLMs (we're not here yet sadly).


I've been cooking with the help of LLMs a few times a month for over a year now. It's worked out well 9/10 times.

It turns out the "average" version of a recipe that's baked into the weights is usually a solid recipe, and they're really good at offering substitutions for things like "I don't have ingredient X" or "make it vegetarian".

It's also fun promoting "make it tastier" once or twice after each recipe just to see what happens.


One of the most satisfying and useful applications for AI is handing it the URL for a recipe (replete with 8 paragraphs of useless introductory text, 2 paragraphs dedicated to how to refrigerate the meal, 9 inline advertisements and a mid-scroll ad) and having it return the actual recipe in Markdown in an Obsidian folder.


Surprisingly, a lot of these recipe websites (even the worst offenders for fluff and ads) have a clean JSON API for getting the recipe directly. I self-host a Mealie instance (https://docs.mealie.io/) which has an "import from URL" feature that uses this. It has rarely if ever failed me.


You can also hit "print" and get a nicely formatted, single page list of ingredients and recipe.


The other day I did that with a TikTok video! It was infuriatingly vague, so I extracted the mp4 using yt-dlp and fed the video file into Gemini Flash and told it to fill in the missing information and give me back a proper recipe.


I have actually cooked with LLMs, specifically Gemini, few times this year.

Works surprisingly well, can even tell it "I have so and so ingredients in my pantry, I want a keto-friendly meal, what can I make?" followed with some narrowing down dialogue-style.


This is definitely controversial, but I think the best CLAUDE.MD is no CLAUDE.MD at all. Every time I found myself wanting to put information into that file, I asked myself "Why isn't this already obvious from the code itself?"

I don't need to tell the agent my tech stack, what DB access library I'm using, the way in which I write my tests, etc. Why? Because it's all very clearly spelled out within the code itself. I've gone great lengths to make sure that my fairly complex domain can be understood by a person with little domain knowledge, which means it should certainly be understood by a tool that has a wealth of it.


I think that's something that has changed in the last 6 months. It used to be standard practice to let Claude scan everything once and describe your repo in the CLAUDE.md - the logic being you don't want to do that every time for every prompt. But now I think the tooling has gotten so good at just grepping around your repos, and maintaining memory from previous sessions, that the best practice is only use CLAUDE.md for things that aren't obvious from the code.


I don't think that is controversial.

My system CLAUDE.MD spells out my preferences in stack and architecture, and how I expect the agent to interact with me.

I have it generate a summary doc in large repos so it doesn't need to slurp in the world to review a PR or work on a corner of it.


Somehow all the with/without cookbook design examples look vibe coded on both sides to me.

I'm not enough of a designer to be able to point exactly at what makes it so, but Claude does seem to have a somewhat limited repertoire of styles.

Maybe if you could point more precisely at the required changes, you could discourage it?


I'm a bit disappointed. Based on the title, I was hoping this was going to be a list of popular recipes Claude has suggested for cooking food. It's one of the not-so-obvious use cases I have a Claude/ChatGPT. It's been really helpful for figuring out how to cook something new (for me). It is easy to ask "how do I cook X" when I have no idea of where to start. Claude has also been helpful when trying to figure out how to substitute different ingredients when I've forgotten something at the store. I've also gotten corrections in recipes, with Claude (correctly) identifying different issues that I didn't notice (like when Claude suggested adding more sugar to a sorbet too avoid having excess ice when freezing in a sorbet).

I can't be bothered to watch videos and courses on how to use claude better when I can simply ask it myself. > I'm either going to ask the AI how to do itFirst you have to know that "it" exists and is possible. A cookbook like this introduces readers to concepts and features they didn't know were there in the first place. It was more important with earlier models, as they were less RL'd to golden paths, so the context could steer them more.Now process engineering is more important than prompt engineering. This is how I've gone about it with my Shopify site.I appreciate there may be more efficient ways to do things if you're an actual developer or engineer but I'm not and the tools I've been able to build so far have been both fun to make and add value to our workflow as a small 2 person ecommerce business.I do at least have a background in web as a designer for many years and having worked with developers I can at least spec and understand how a product might work which is one thing Claude/AI isn't hugely helpful with and often it's the testing and QA phase as always where the problems and shortcomings expose themselves. Ok, thanks, because I looked at one example and was like, “I am supposed to take someone’s OpenAPI doc and translate it back to English for the model?” And if they’re implying I should have an AI do that, why don’t they just build that step into the models rather than having someone prompt an AI to write these half-ass docs? if you know how to ask Ai about how to use Ai, you already know how to use Aithe point of guides is to provide assurance for people unfamiliar to the process in the first place If the bar is knowing how to type a question into a box, I'm confident almost everyone is better off starting with that then reading a "cookbook" that starts with installing python packages. I've see people write "broooo pleaseeee!" into the box. Something about the universe providing better fools. I think the main thing of interest in the linked site is the dates. You can quickly get a view of what was possible and when. You don't learn about progress if you don't take part in it.Before /goal was ralph-wiggum it was def an interesting learning experience, it was interesting to see how Claude became a lot better doing this itself but it still took 6 Month and more.You can wait and sit it out and suddenly you get fired if you miss the point when to start spending more time and energy on topics like this. I've been using Matt Pocock's skills at home, and they seem pretty great. He makes a great case for them vs other skills in his videos. I don't remember the exact arguments, but basically they're designed to be called by the user instead of called automatically, and that makes them take less context merely because they exist.I've also found that they tend to coerce the programmer into thinking about the end result, rather than try to push them out of that role. His grill skills are about making sure the actual requirements are known, and his prototype skill can help explore things that need to be experienced to make a hard decision on. Fascinating! Makes me wonder if they were chosen to maximize the contrast between the before and after shots, just to "prove that it "works"".Personally I prefer the before shots all the way down. I was hoping too, because I use a Claude project for recipes and it’s great for suggestions and adapting recipes for what’s in the pantry. Does anybody here have a frontend development workflow that works well with coding agents? In my experience coding agents ship buggy, broken, incomplete, or awkward frontend features at a way higher frequency than they do for backend features. The reason seems obviously to be the difference in verifiability between the two. Basic test suites dont seem to help much. I assume something like Garry Tan’s gstack is the right direction but I dont know if that particular tool is mature enough to adopt yet. I was surprised too see here on HN recently that gemini 3.5 flash may outperform Opus/gpt-5.5 on frontend tasks (maybe because of Gemini’s supposed edge w.r.t. multimodality? or maybe it understands Chrome more deeply?), can anyone here second that take? There may be good options coupled with agents like Claudes Front End Design skill, I haven't used it.I have been using Magic Patterns which is very good at generating initial prototypes, especially when driven by an agent familiar with it's facilities and constraints.I assume the service is just a wrapper plus scaffolding but I like it. You have to push it though to get anything particularly creative but it's good at standard front end designs.https://www.magicpatterns.com/That's not necessarily what you're after because I only use it for piloting stuff, not for adhoc feature addition and fixes. Agents aren't great at complex async state updates, mostly because it's hard to represent what's happening in their context. They're fine at creating complex components in isolation and implementing decoupled stuff like animations. After all this effort of not opening the models, then trying to forbid people to use open models and all the rubbish their CEO keeps talking about common people I am done with Anthropic.I can't use it on good faith Same goes for grok, chat gpt and others all involved in killing people and monopolising the market. That sounds like a great idea. Let's stop killing people altogether. It's almost entirely unnecessary. I thought it would be a litteral cookbook on how to make plausible/feasible recipes with LLMs (we're not here yet sadly). I've been cooking with the help of LLMs a few times a month for over a year now. It's worked out well 9/10 times.It turns out the "average" version of a recipe that's baked into the weights is usually a solid recipe, and they're really good at offering substitutions for things like "I don't have ingredient X" or "make it vegetarian".It's also fun promoting "make it tastier" once or twice after each recipe just to see what happens. One of the most satisfying and useful applications for AI is handing it the URL for a recipe (replete with 8 paragraphs of useless introductory text, 2 paragraphs dedicated to how to refrigerate the meal, 9 inline advertisements and a mid-scroll ad) and having it return the actual recipe in Markdown in an Obsidian folder. Surprisingly, a lot of these recipe websites (even the worst offenders for fluff and ads) have a clean JSON API for getting the recipe directly. I self-host a Mealie instance (https://docs.mealie.io/) which has an "import from URL" feature that uses this. It has rarely if ever failed me. You can also hit "print" and get a nicely formatted, single page list of ingredients and recipe. The other day I did that with a TikTok video! It was infuriatingly vague, so I extracted the mp4 using yt-dlp and fed the video file into Gemini Flash and told it to fill in the missing information and give me back a proper recipe. I have actually cooked with LLMs, specifically Gemini, few times this year.Works surprisingly well, can even tell it "I have so and so ingredients in my pantry, I want a keto-friendly meal, what can I make?" followed with some narrowing down dialogue-style. This is definitely controversial, but I think the best CLAUDE.MD is no CLAUDE.MD at all. Every time I found myself wanting to put information into that file, I asked myself "Why isn't this already obvious from the code itself?"I don't need to tell the agent my tech stack, what DB access library I'm using, the way in which I write my tests, etc. Why? Because it's all very clearly spelled out within the code itself. I've gone great lengths to make sure that my fairly complex domain can be understood by a person with little domain knowledge, which means it should certainly be understood by a tool that has a wealth of it. I think that's something that has changed in the last 6 months. It used to be standard practice to let Claude scan everything once and describe your repo in the CLAUDE.md - the logic being you don't want to do that every time for every prompt. But now I think the tooling has gotten so good at just grepping around your repos, and maintaining memory from previous sessions, that the best practice is only use CLAUDE.md for things that aren't obvious from the code. I don't think that is controversial.My system CLAUDE.MD spells out my preferences in stack and architecture, and how I expect the agent to interact with me.I have it generate a summary doc in large repos so it doesn't need to slurp in the world to review a PR or work on a corner of it. Somehow all the with/without cookbook design examples look vibe coded on both sides to me.I'm not enough of a designer to be able to point exactly at what makes it so, but Claude does seem to have a somewhat limited repertoire of styles.Maybe if you could point more precisely at the required changes, you could discourage it? I'm a bit disappointed. Based on the title, I was hoping this was going to be a list of popular recipes Claude has suggested for cooking food. It's one of the not-so-obvious use cases I have a Claude/ChatGPT. It's been really helpful for figuring out how to cook something new (for me). It is easy to ask "how do I cook X" when I have no idea of where to start. Claude has also been helpful when trying to figure out how to substitute different ingredients when I've forgotten something at the store. I've also gotten corrections in recipes, with Claude (correctly) identifying different issues that I didn't notice (like when Claude suggested adding more sugar to a sorbet too avoid having excess ice when freezing in a sorbet).

Gotta be honest, almost every "how to use AI" resource seems pointless to me. I'm either going to ask the AI how to do it, or if it's about using the AI then we can just bake it into the harness or wait for Anthropic/OpenAI to do it for me because they're always trivial.

All of these resources on agentic workflows, managing agent memory, harness engineering, etc. appear to just be theatre to me.

blocks.html_en:
Remember in 2023 when people thought "prompt engineering" would be the new software engineering and invested tons of time into learning CoT, ReAct, thread-of-thoughts, etc?

Those were mostly obviated by reasoning models and harness updates by 2024.

It seems pointless to invest energy into the latest/greatest AI technique or framework when they're going to either be absorbed or replaced on a 3 month cycle.

blocks.html_en: Isn't it clear that some people are better at working with/prompting LLMs than other people? Or is the idea that what you write to them and how you use them doesn't matter, it's all up to the model/harness? To me this seems clear, so then clearly this is a skill, which typically is called "prompt engineering". Specifically CoT or the other things you mention wasn't referred to as "prompt engineering" as far as I know, that skill is more about how you communicate with the LLMs and how you use them, rather than what specific processes/workflows/technologies you use.

blocks.html_en:
I actually think that good prompting MOSTLY comes from good writing skills in general. Being able to more clearly state things to an agent, knowing what pieces of context are entirely unnecessary and which are important, having a larger vocabulary helps too.

Of course, there are other areas that can improve model output (Direction rather than open-ended assistance requests, using keywords + plugins that help, the "your output should include: " style prompting).

A few of us run almost the same exact setup at my shop (Base Claude Code w/ SuperPowers + a context repository) and the models are somewhat unhelpful to some, and give meaningful output to others. The only correlation I notice is that their prompts are no-good. Not from a meta "prompt" engineering standpoint, but from a general English 101 standpoint.

"dudde no i wanted the function to return 3 things. not like that. do it again"

VS something like

"Modify the "renderThreeVars()" function signature to accept another variable called "z" and add it to the return statement at line 64."

Obvious exaggeration, but you get the point.

sentences.en: I actually think that good prompting MOSTLY comes from good writing skills in general.
sentences.zh: 实际上，我认为良好的提示主要来自于良好的写作技巧。

sentences.en: Being able to more clearly state things to an agent, knowing what pieces of context are entirely unnecessary and which are important, having a larger vocabulary helps too.Of course, there are other areas that can improve model output (Direction rather than open-ended assistance requests, using keywords + plugins that help, the "your output should include: " style prompting).A few of us run almost the same exact setup at my shop (Base Claude Code w/ SuperPowers + a context repository) and the models are somewhat unhelpful to some, and give meaningful output to others.
sentences.zh: 能够更清楚地向代理陈述事情，知道哪些上下文是完全不必要的，哪些是重要的，拥有更大的词汇量也有帮助。当然，还有其他领域可以改进模型输出（方向而不是开放式协助请求，使用有帮助的关键字+插件，“你的输出应该包括：”风格提示）。我们中的一些人在我的商店运行几乎相同的设置（带有 SuperPowers + 上下文存储库的 Base Claude 代码），并且模型对某些人来说有些无用，并且给对他人有意义的输出。

sentences.en: The only correlation I notice is that their prompts are no-good.
sentences.zh: 我注意到的唯一关联是他们的提示不好。

sentences.en: Not from a meta "prompt" engineering standpoint, but from a general English 101 standpoint."dudde no i wanted the function to return 3 things.
sentences.zh: 不是从元“提示”工程的角度来看，而是从一般英语 101 的角度来看。“老兄，不，我希望函数返回 3 件事。

sentences.en: not like that.
sentences.zh: 不是那样的。

sentences.en: do it again"VS something like"Modify the "renderThreeVars()" function signature to accept another variable called "z" and add it to the return statement at line 64."Obvious exaggeration, but you get the point.
sentences.zh: 再做一次“VS类似“修改“renderThreeVars()”函数签名以接受另一个名为“z”的变量并将其添加到第64行的return语句中。”明显夸张，但你明白了。

blocks.html_en:
Why not open-ended assistance requests?

I ask it all the time about whether X is feasible, how we can get started on Y, and to investigate issue Z.

It is working great for me in a >100k LOC project.

Perhaps this works less well with weaker models. I suspect the people who say Qwen 3.6 27B is working well, are using prompts like "modify the renderThreeVars() function in rendering.py".

blocks.html_en:
As part of a previous job I needed to audit internal AI usage from a largely non-technical employee population.

The prompts were, predictably, really bad. Broken English, sentence fragments, vague requests, lack of context. Yet somehow, the users always got the answer they were looking for. It might have taken a few extra turns with questions from the model, but the end result was the same.

It's humbling, but a flowery, carefully crafted prompt is at best slightly more efficient than a "CAN A DOG BE EATIN SUN FLOWER SEED?" peasant prompt.

sentences.zh: 作为之前工作的一部分，我需要审核大部分非技术员工群体的内部人工智能使用情况。不出所料，提示非常糟糕。

sentences.en: Broken English, sentence fragments, vague requests, lack of context.
sentences.zh: 英语蹩脚、句子碎片、要求模糊、缺乏上下文。

sentences.en: Yet somehow, the users always got the answer they were looking for.
sentences.zh: 然而不知何故，用户总能得到他们正在寻找的答案。

sentences.en: It might have taken a few extra turns with questions from the model, but the end result was the same.It's humbling, but a flowery, carefully crafted prompt is at best slightly more efficient than a "CAN A DOG BE EATIN SUN FLOWER SEED?"
sentences.zh: 模型的问题可能需要多花几个回合，但最终的结果是一样的。这令人谦卑，但一个华丽的、精心设计的提示最多比“狗可以吃向日葵种子吗？”稍微有效一点。

blocks.html_en: To be pedantic, these types of prompts work best when in first-person/roleplaying. So the "perfect" prompt here would be something like, "I'm a dog and I just ate 20 grams of salted sunflower seeds with the shell on. Because I'm a dog I sometimes eat things without thinking about it. I'm worried about the short and long-term physiological consequences of what I've just done..."

blocks.html_en:
I copied your prompt verbatim to ChatGPT and to Google.

ChatGPT kept the charade for all of one sentence. Then it dropped to talking about "your dog" the rest of the way. It even starts the final paragraph with "if, instead, you mean you (a human) ate them...", and finishes with the question "is this about an actual dog or yourself?"

Google did consistently refer to me as a dog, but its entire focus was on the steps "your human" should take, no advice for the dog itself.

In both cases, it looks like the first person roleplay was entirely inconsequential for the usefulness of the output. I think the current gen AIs have outgrown this trick and you can safely forget about it.

sentences.en: I copied your prompt verbatim to ChatGPT and to Google.ChatGPT kept the charade for all of one sentence.
sentences.zh: 我将您的提示逐字复制到 ChatGPT 和 Google。ChatGPT 保留了整个句子的谜语。

sentences.en: Then it dropped to talking about "your dog" the rest of the way.
sentences.zh: 然后剩下的时间就变成了谈论“你的狗”。

sentences.en: It even starts the final paragraph with "if, instead, you mean you (a human) ate them...", and finishes with the question "is this about an actual dog or yourself?"Google did consistently refer to me as a dog, but its entire focus was on the steps "your human" should take, no advice for the dog itself.In both cases, it looks like the first person roleplay was entirely inconsequential for the usefulness of the output.
sentences.zh: 它甚至以“如果，相反，你的意思是你（人类）吃了它们......”开始，并以问题“这是关于一只真正的狗还是你自己？”结束，谷歌确实一直将我称为狗，但它的全部重点是“你的人类”应该采取的步骤，对狗本身没有建议。在这两种情况下，看起来第一人称角色扮演对于输出的有用性完全无关紧要。

sentences.en: I think the current gen AIs have outgrown this trick and you can safely forget about it.
sentences.zh: 我认为当前的人工智能已经不再需要这个技巧了，你可以放心地忘记它。

I feel the same way about "prompt engineering" as I feel regarding the term "parkour" - you know, running and jumping on stuff.

Are people really putting on their resumes that they are capable of reading and writing and appropriately defining and limiting context? That's all prompt engineering is - it's being able to communicate effectively and elucidate your objectives.

Congratulations to all you English majors out there, you're about to make $350K/year.

blocks.html_en:
It's not really a skill. The models are at this point smarter than you are, so the idea that you can prompt them "better" is laughable really when discussing frontier models.

It's like imagining you could "prompt" Richard Feynman to be smarter at Physics.

That is, for 99.9% of engineers, if you want the model to do a code review of your project, the best solution is to just ask Fable, "Hey Fable, do a code review of this project." Throwing in extra text like "think like a senior engineer", "ensure you focus on DRY principles, KISS, self documenting code, etc", doesn't make a difference.

These sorts of tricks used to work with dumber models, but now, like I said before, it's like thinking you can prompt Linus Torvalds into writing better C than he already can do.

sentences.en: It's not really a skill.
sentences.zh: 这并不是真正的技能。

sentences.en: The models are at this point smarter than you are, so the idea that you can prompt them "better" is laughable really when discussing frontier models.It's like imagining you could "prompt" Richard Feynman to be smarter at Physics.That is, for 99.9% of engineers, if you want the model to do a code review of your project, the best solution is to just ask Fable, "Hey Fable, do a code review of this project."
sentences.zh: 在这一点上，模型比你更聪明，所以在讨论前沿模型时，你可以提示他们“更好”的想法确实很可笑。这就像想象你可以“提示”Richard Feynman 在物理学上更聪明。也就是说，对于 99.9% 的工程师来说，如果你希望模型对你的项目进行代码审查，最好的解决方案就是问 Fable，“嘿 Fable，对这个项目进行代码审查。”

sentences.en: Throwing in extra text like "think like a senior engineer", "ensure you focus on DRY principles, KISS, self documenting code, etc", doesn't make a difference.These sorts of tricks used to work with dumber models, but now, like I said before, it's like thinking you can prompt Linus Torvalds into writing better C than he already can do.
sentences.zh: 添加额外的文本，如“像高级工程师一样思考”、“确保你专注于 DRY 原则、KISS、自文档化代码等”，并没有什么区别。这些技巧过去常常用于较笨的模型，但现在，就像我之前说过的，这就像认为你可以促使 Linus Torvalds 编写比他已经能做的更好的 C 语言。

FWiW

> it's like thinking you can prompt Linus Torvalds into writing better C++ than he already can do.

  Linus Torvalds, the inventor (and beloved dictator) of Linux, has always been quite harsh about C++ and why he rejects it for Linux kernel development. He’s not just been very vocal about it, but also brought up some arguments against the use of C++ that are worth reviewing in detail.

~ https://medium.com/@jankammerath/linus-torvalds-critique-of-...

blocks.html_en:
I mean, the point stands.

The models are beyond expert level in many areas at this point.

Do you really believe that adding extra junk to your prompt is going to make the model write code better than it does already?

blocks.html_en: There was an article by Vercel on how ineffective tool calling is compared to just a single md file with clearly defined instructions. They showed a clever way on how to use compressed indexes. My experience with tool calling was similar to what Vercel described, and I spent hours trying to perfect it. Since reading Vercel’s findings, Claude.md and Agents.md, maybe a Project.md it’s all I use.

blocks.html_en:
Yes, but I have had moderate luck with creating an "agent-instructor" skill that has strict instructions around keeping language strong, unambiguous, concise, and always presenting me with exact diffs to review before writing anything.

Another thing in it is a strict line count. Any increase in line count requires my approval. That last one is important because it plays well with two biases: models don't tend to create long lines so they won't try to cheat that way, and they're strongly inclined to keep churning out lines so I take that away from them.

sentences.en: Any increase in line count requires my approval.
sentences.zh: 行数的任何增加都需要我的批准。

Thanks for saying the quiet part out loud. Everytime I do a demo it seems like a waste of time compared to just actually building.

Unless I'm doing something super complicated even taking time to set things up like subagents, etc. seems like a waste compared to just building.

The only things that really seem beneficial (for Claude Code) seems to be learning to set up loops, memory and finding relevant MCP servers.

It was more important with earlier models, as they were less RL'd to golden paths, so the context could steer them more.

Now process engineering is more important than prompt engineering.

I appreciate there may be more efficient ways to do things if you're an actual developer or engineer but I'm not and the tools I've been able to build so far have been both fun to make and add value to our workflow as a small 2 person ecommerce business.

I do at least have a background in web as a designer for many years and having worked with developers I can at least spec and understand how a product might work which is one thing Claude/AI isn't hugely helpful with and often it's the testing and QA phase as always where the problems and shortcomings expose themselves.

You don't learn about progress if you don't take part in it.

Before /goal was ralph-wiggum it was def an interesting learning experience, it was interesting to see how Claude became a lot better doing this itself but it still took 6 Month and more.

You can wait and sit it out and suddenly you get fired if you miss the point when to start spending more time and energy on topics like this.

sentences.zh: 如果你不参与其中，你就不会了解进展。在 /goal 成为 ralph-wiggum 之前，这确实是一次有趣的学习经历，很有趣的是看到 Claude 如何在这方面做得更好，但仍然需要 6 个月或更长时间。你可以等待并袖手旁观，如果你错过了开始在此类主题上花费更多时间和精力的时间点，你会突然被解雇。

blocks.html_en:
I've been using Matt Pocock's skills at home, and they seem pretty great. He makes a great case for them vs other skills in his videos. I don't remember the exact arguments, but basically they're designed to be called by the user instead of called automatically, and that makes them take less context merely because they exist.

I've also found that they tend to coerce the programmer into thinking about the end result, rather than try to push them out of that role. His grill skills are about making sure the actual requirements are known, and his prototype skill can help explore things that need to be experienced to make a hard decision on.

sentences.en: I've been using Matt Pocock's skills at home, and they seem pretty great.
sentences.zh: 我一直在家里使用 Matt Pocock 的技能，它们看起来非常棒。

sentences.en: He makes a great case for them vs other skills in his videos.
sentences.zh: 他在视频中对这些技能与其他技能进行了很好的论证。

sentences.en: I don't remember the exact arguments, but basically they're designed to be called by the user instead of called automatically, and that makes them take less context merely because they exist.I've also found that they tend to coerce the programmer into thinking about the end result, rather than try to push them out of that role.
sentences.zh: 我不记得确切的参数，但基本上它们被设计为由用户调用而不是自动调用，这使得它们仅仅因为它们的存在而需要更少的上下文。我还发现它们倾向于强迫程序员思考最终结果，而不是试图将他们推出该角色。

sentences.en: His grill skills are about making sure the actual requirements are known, and his prototype skill can help explore things that need to be experienced to make a hard decision on.
sentences.zh: 他的烧烤技能是为了确保了解实际要求，而他的原型技能可以帮助探索需要经历的事情才能做出艰难的决定。

blocks.html_en: Does anybody here have a frontend development workflow that works well with coding agents? In my experience coding agents ship buggy, broken, incomplete, or awkward frontend features at a way higher frequency than they do for backend features. The reason seems obviously to be the difference in verifiability between the two. Basic test suites dont seem to help much. I assume something like Garry Tan’s gstack is the right direction but I dont know if that particular tool is mature enough to adopt yet. I was surprised too see here on HN recently that gemini 3.5 flash may outperform Opus/gpt-5.5 on frontend tasks (maybe because of Gemini’s supposed edge w.r.t. multimodality? or maybe it understands Chrome more deeply?), can anyone here second that take?

There may be good options coupled with agents like Claudes Front End Design skill, I haven't used it.

I have been using Magic Patterns which is very good at generating initial prototypes, especially when driven by an agent familiar with it's facilities and constraints.

I assume the service is just a wrapper plus scaffolding but I like it. You have to push it though to get anything particularly creative but it's good at standard front end designs.

https://www.magicpatterns.com/

That's not necessarily what you're after because I only use it for piloting stuff, not for adhoc feature addition and fixes.

blocks.html_en:
I've been cooking with the help of LLMs a few times a month for over a year now. It's worked out well 9/10 times.

It turns out the "average" version of a recipe that's baked into the weights is usually a solid recipe, and they're really good at offering substitutions for things like "I don't have ingredient X" or "make it vegetarian".

It's also fun promoting "make it tastier" once or twice after each recipe just to see what happens.

sentences.en: I've been cooking with the help of LLMs a few times a month for over a year now.
sentences.zh: 一年多以来，我每个月都会在法学硕士的帮助下做饭几次。

sentences.en: It's worked out well 9/10 times.It turns out the "average" version of a recipe that's baked into the weights is usually a solid recipe, and they're really good at offering substitutions for things like "I don't have ingredient X" or "make it vegetarian".It's also fun promoting "make it tastier" once or twice after each recipe just to see what happens.
sentences.zh: 9/10 的结果很好。事实证明，烘焙到重量中的食谱的“平均”版本通常是可靠的食谱，而且他们非常擅长提供诸如“我没有 X 成分”或“做成素食”之类的替代品。在每个食谱之后宣传“让它更美味”一两次，看看会发生什么也很有趣。

blocks.html_en:
This is definitely controversial, but I think the best CLAUDE.MD is no CLAUDE.MD at all. Every time I found myself wanting to put information into that file, I asked myself "Why isn't this already obvious from the code itself?"

I don't need to tell the agent my tech stack, what DB access library I'm using, the way in which I write my tests, etc. Why? Because it's all very clearly spelled out within the code itself. I've gone great lengths to make sure that my fairly complex domain can be understood by a person with little domain knowledge, which means it should certainly be understood by a tool that has a wealth of it.

blocks.html_en: I think that's something that has changed in the last 6 months. It used to be standard practice to let Claude scan everything once and describe your repo in the CLAUDE.md - the logic being you don't want to do that every time for every prompt. But now I think the tooling has gotten so good at just grepping around your repos, and maintaining memory from previous sessions, that the best practice is only use CLAUDE.md for things that aren't obvious from the code.

blocks.html_en:
I don't think that is controversial.

My system CLAUDE.MD spells out my preferences in stack and architecture, and how I expect the agent to interact with me.

I have it generate a summary doc in large repos so it doesn't need to slurp in the world to review a PR or work on a corner of it.

sentences.en: I don't think that is controversial.My system CLAUDE.MD spells out my preferences in stack and architecture, and how I expect the agent to interact with me.I have it generate a summary doc in large repos so it doesn't need to slurp in the world to review a PR or work on a corner of it.
sentences.zh: 我不认为这是有争议的。我的系统 CLAUDE.MD 阐明了我在堆栈和架构方面的偏好，以及我期望代理如何与我交互。我让它在大型存储库中生成一个摘要文档，这样它就不需要花时间去审查 PR 或在它的某个角落工作。

blocks.html_en:
Somehow all the with/without cookbook design examples look vibe coded on both sides to me.

I'm not enough of a designer to be able to point exactly at what makes it so, but Claude does seem to have a somewhat limited repertoire of styles.

Maybe if you could point more precisely at the required changes, you could discourage it?

sentences.en: Somehow all the with/without cookbook design examples look vibe coded on both sides to me.I'm not enough of a designer to be able to point exactly at what makes it so, but Claude does seem to have a somewhat limited repertoire of styles.Maybe if you could point more precisely at the required changes, you could discourage it?
sentences.zh: 不知何故，所有带/不带食谱的设计示例对我来说看起来都是编码的。我不是一个设计师，无法准确指出是什么造成了这种情况，但克劳德似乎确实有一些有限的风格。也许如果你能更准确地指出所需的更改，你可以阻止它吗？

content_html_zh: 老实说，几乎所有“如何使用人工智能”资源对我来说似乎毫无意义。 我要么询问人工智能如何做到这一点，要么如果是关于使用人工智能，那么我们可以将其烘焙到线束中，或者等待 Anthropic/OpenAI 为我做这件事，因为它们总是微不足道的。所有这些关于代理工作流程、管理代理内存、线束工程等的资源对我来说似乎只是戏剧。还记得在 2023 年，人们认为“即时工程”将是新的软件工程，并投入大量时间来学习 CoT、ReAct、思路等？到 2024 年，这些大部分都被推理模型和利用更新所消除。当最新/最伟大的人工智能技术或框架将在 3 个月的周期内被吸收或替换时，将精力投入到最新/最伟大的人工智能技术或框架上似乎毫无意义。难道不是很明显有些人比其他人更擅长与法学硕士合作/提示他们吗？ 或者你写给他们的内容以及如何使用它们并不重要，这完全取决于模型/线束？ 对我来说，这似乎很清楚，所以很明显这是一种技能，通常被称为“即时工程”。 具体来说，据我所知，CoT 或您提到的其他内容并不被称为“即时工程”，该技能更多的是关于您如何与 LLM 沟通以及如何使用它们，而不是您使用的具体流程/工作流程/技术。实际上，我认为良好的提示主要来自于良好的写作技巧。 能够更清楚地向代理陈述事情，知道哪些上下文是完全不必要的，哪些是重要的，拥有更大的词汇量也有帮助。当然，还有其他领域可以改进模型输出（方向而不是开放式协助请求，使用有帮助的关键字+插件，“你的输出应该包括：”风格提示）。我们中的一些人在我的商店运行几乎相同的设置（带有 SuperPowers + 上下文存储库的 Base Claude 代码），并且模型对某些人来说有些无用，并且给对他人有意义的输出。 我注意到的唯一关联是他们的提示不好。 不是从元“提示”工程的角度来看，而是从一般英语 101 的角度来看。“老兄，不，我希望函数返回 3 件事。 不是那样的。 再做一次“VS类似“修改“renderThreeVars()”函数签名以接受另一个名为“z”的变量并将其添加到第64行的return语句中。”明显夸张，但你明白了。为什么不提出开放式援助请求？我总是问它 X 是否可行，我们如何开始 Y，以及调查问题 Z。它在 >100k LOC 项目中对我来说非常有效。也许这对于较弱的模型效果不太好。 我怀疑那些说Qwen 3.6 27B运行良好的人正在使用诸如“修改rendering.py中的renderThreeVars()函数”之类的提示。作为之前工作的一部分，我需要审核大部分非技术员工群体的内部人工智能使用情况。不出所料，提示非常糟糕。 英语蹩脚、句子碎片、要求模糊、缺乏上下文。 然而不知何故，用户总能得到他们正在寻找的答案。 模型的问题可能需要多花几个回合，但最终的结果是一样的。这令人谦卑，但一个华丽的、精心设计的提示最多比“狗可以吃向日葵种子吗？”稍微有效一点。 农民提示。你称其为农民提示，但实际上它几乎是完美的。 有几个人注意到，但已经完成了 95%。 “狗能吃葵花籽吗”可能是完美的版本，这个版本中只有1个无关的词。除非用户想知道猫是否可以吃葵花籽之类的。迂腐地说，这些类型的提示在第一人称/角色扮演中效果最好。 所以这里“完美”的提示应该是这样的：“我是一只狗，我刚刚吃了 20 克带壳的咸葵花籽。 因为我是一只狗，所以有时我会不假思索地吃东西。 我担心我刚刚所做的事情会产生短期和长期的生理后果……”我将您的提示逐字复制到 ChatGPT 和 Google。ChatGPT 保留了整个句子的谜语。 然后剩下的时间就变成了谈论“你的狗”。 它甚至以“如果，相反，你的意思是你（人类）吃了它们......”开始，并以问题“这是关于一只真正的狗还是你自己？”结束，谷歌确实一直将我称为狗，但它的全部重点是“你的人类”应该采取的步骤，对狗本身没有建议。在这两种情况下，看起来第一人称角色扮演对于输出的有用性完全无关紧要。 我认为当前的人工智能已经不再需要这个技巧了，你可以放心地忘记它。我认为，一些即使没有领域知识也能更好地“提示”的人，可能比那些拥有良好领域知识但没有良好提示技能的人更能让LLM代理人产生良好的结果。 不过，这只是一个假设，有时间尝试一下会很有趣:)我对“快速工程”的感受与我对“跑酷”一词的感受相同——你知道，在东西上奔跑和跳跃。人们真的在简历中表明他们有能力阅读和写作，并适当地定义和限制背景吗？ 这就是即时工程的全部内容 - 它能够有效地沟通并阐明您的目标。恭喜所有英语专业的学生，​​您即将赚取 35 万美元/年。显而易见的是，围绕法学硕士有很多炒作，以前因 IC 而受到重视的人现在都从事先令生意。强化学习基本上已经扼杀了即时工程。 您仍然需要提供正确的背景和流程，但除此之外如何与他们沟通不再那么重要。> 还是认为您写给它们的内容以及如何使用它们并不重要，这完全取决于模型/线束？是的，确实如此。 资料来源：让模型从头开始实现复杂的东西，无论是一行提示还是详细的“保证有效的 SOTA 巫术魔法咒语”这并不是真正的技能。 在这一点上，模型比你更聪明，所以在讨论前沿模型时，你可以提示他们“更好”的想法确实很可笑。这就像想象你可以“提示”Richard Feynman 在物理学上更聪明。也就是说，对于 99.9% 的工程师来说，如果你希望模型对你的项目进行代码审查，最好的解决方案就是问 Fable，“嘿 Fable，对这个项目进行代码审查。” 添加额外的文本，如“像高级工程师一样思考”、“确保你专注于 DRY 原则、KISS、自文档化代码等”，并没有什么区别。这些技巧过去常常用于较笨的模型，但现在，就像我之前说过的，这就像认为你可以促使 Linus Torvalds 编写比他已经能做的更好的 C 语言。“DRY”和“KISS”可能是软件设计者可能遵守的最无趣的原则。FWiW> 这就像认为你可以促使 Linus Torvalds 编写比他已经能做的更好的 C++。 Linux 的发明者（也是受人爱戴的独裁者）Linus Torvalds 一直对 C++ 非常严厉，也解释了为什么他拒绝使用 C++ 来开发 Linux 内核。 他不仅对此非常直言不讳，而且还提出了一些反对使用 C++ 的论点，这些论点值得详细回顾。 〜 https://medium.com/@jankammerath/linus-torvalds-critique-of-...我的意思是，这一点是正确的。目前，模型在许多领域都超出了专家水平。你真的相信在提示中添加额外的垃圾会让模型编写代码比现在更好吗？再次，想象一下去陶哲轩并“提示”他在数学方面取得更好的成绩，你认为你能做到吗？ 你会给他什么提示，让他学出更好的数学？ 除非你已经是世界领先的数学家，否则我认为你会发现这很难。> 我的意思是，这一点是正确的。 目前，这些模型在许多领域都超出了专家水平。这并不是说这些模型不智能或其他什么；而是说这些模型不聪明。我们知道他们非常有能力。能够清楚地传达您希望模型做什么总是有价值的，尤其是在缺乏上下文的情况下。我发现这在我的实践和测试中非常不真实，其中寓言提示进行审查发现了表面问题，而按照“假设它是错误的，证明它是正确的”的提示发现了更深入和真正的问题。我仍然相信写出好的提示或好的说明。 糟糕的提示有时会导致账单花销。 写得不好的规范可能会浪费很多代币确实。 但是如何与某个模型对话的特殊知识通常是不值得的。能够写清楚，总是值得的。Vercel 有一篇文章介绍了如何将无效的工具调用与具有明确定义指令的单个 md 文件进行比较。 他们展示了如何使用压缩索引的巧妙方法。 我对工具调用的体验与 Vercel 描述的类似，我花了几个小时试图完善它。 自从阅读了 Vercel 的发现、Claude.md 和 Agents.md，也许是 Project.md，这就是我所使用的全部。现在，我觉得我从每天与《神鬼寓言》一起工作中获得了很多价值，但我会不好意思分享我的课程，因为它们几乎就是我与同事交谈的方式。 包括拼写错误和不成熟的想法。 我 6 个月前自豪地写下的那些正式的提示和规范已经消失了。 但它正在得到结果。不仅如此，所有围绕模型的工具的保质期都很短，因为模型本身的功能不断增强，它们会吸收这些工具。 我们已经一遍又一遍地看到它。阿门，感谢您和OP的评论。这些工具开箱即用，效果很好。 我确信我可以挤出更好的令牌使用或简化一些工具调用，但这不是我真正想要关注的事情。 就像我不想无休止地配置我的 IDE 一样，除了实际构建某些东西之外，我真的没有耐心花时间在任何事情上。>我要么会问人工智能如何做到这一点LLMs似乎很不擅长在安全带中使用LLMs。 你有没有看到，如果你允许的话，他们如何用他们放入 .md 文件中的内容来破坏他们的上下文？你必须让法学硕士进行搜索，因此这些资源可能比你更适合他们是的，但我在创建“代理-指导者”技能方面运气不错，该技能对保持语言的强大、明确、简洁有严格的指示，并且在编写任何内容之前总是向我提供精确的差异以供审阅。其中的另一件事是严格的行数。 行数的任何增加都需要我的批准。 最后一个很重要，因为它很好地解决了两个偏见：模型不倾向于创建长线，因此他们不会试图以这种方式作弊，并且他们强烈倾向于继续大量生产线，因此我将其从他们那里拿走。你是否告诉克劳德“制定一个特工教练技能，无论你认为什么好，就去做”，或者你是否使用了你所获得的关于人工智能如何工作以及如何为其编写良好指令的知识？我怀疑某些工作流程（例如 langchain 和其他类似的工作流程）将在未来保留有用性。 在 llm 之前和之后采取确定性步骤是解决任何可能有害的问题的方法。 我猜这就是线束的作用，但是当我们可以使用它来制作满足我们需求的特定线束时，为什么要局限于通用线束呢？感谢您大声说出安静的部分。 每次我做一个演示，与实际构建相比，似乎都是浪费时间。除非我正在做一些超级复杂的事情，甚至花时间设置子代理等，与仅仅构建相比，似乎都是浪费。唯一看起来真正有益的事情（对于克劳德代码）似乎是学习设置循环、内存和查找相关的 MCP 服务器。确切地。 我认为我们都必须自己得出这个结论，因为我们从 LLM 公司得到的信息是“亲爱的用户，你确实为 LLM
